summary: The suite follows the mandated act-based end-to-end approach and uses exact-value assertions (specific counts, percentages, per-file table rows), which is the highest-value thing a test suite can do. The lazy-caching wrappers (getFullFixtureResult / getPassOnlyResult) are a pragmatic way to avoid redundant 90-second act invocations while still letting each assertion live in its own test case. However, the suite has several meaningful gaps: (1) No unit tests at all for parseJUnitXML, parseJSONResults, aggregateTotals, detectFlakyTests, or generateMarkdownSummary — any logic bug that happens to be obscured by the integrated fixture numbers would be missed. (2) Error-handling paths (unsupported file extension, malformed XML/JSON, empty directory) are completely untested despite being an explicit requirement. (3) yaml-parser.ts is relied on by the workflow-structure tests but never tested independently, so a regression there could silently invalidate those assertions. (4) Shared mutable module-level variables (fullFixtureResult, passOnlyResult) create implicit test-ordering dependencies and make it impossible to run a single test in isolation. (5) The pass-only scenario only covers JSON format; no act run exercises an XML-only matrix. (6) writeFileSync(ACT_RESULT_FILE, '') is buried inside a lazy getter rather than a beforeAll hook, which is a design smell. Overall the integration assertions are solid where they exist, but the absence of unit tests and error-path coverage leaves significant regression risk.
